question: [File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to AccountRoutes at '/:idOrAliasOrEvmAddress/tokens' with an idOrAliasOrEvmAddress value of '0x' followed by a null byte (%00) to cause the requestPathLabel to contain a null byte, potentially truncating log entries or causing issues in downstream string processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a high volume of concurrent requests to all three routers (AccountRoutes, BlockRoutes, ContractRoutes) simultaneously to exploit the shared res.locals object and the `!res.locals[requestPathLabel]` guard, causing a race condition where the wrong router's path is recorded for a given request?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to BlockRoutes at '/' (root path) so that req.route.path is '/' and after the `.replace(/\\/+$/g, '')` regex, the requestPathLabel becomes req.baseUrl with no trailing path, potentially matching a broader set of per-path headers in responseHandler.js than intended?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the extendExpress wrapArgs() function's handling of recordRequestPath (which has 2 parameters, not 3) to cause the wrap() function to miscalculate isErrorHandler (fn.length === 4 is false, fn.length === 2 is also not 3), resulting in the wrapped middleware not receiving the correct next argument and silently dropping errors?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to ContractRoutes at '/:contractId' with a contractId value that is a valid EVM address starting with '0x' followed by characters that, when concatenated with req.baseUrl in recordRequestPath, produce a requestPathLabel that matches a sensitive per-path header configuration key in responseHandler.js?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with a crafted X-Forwarded-Prefix header that Express uses to set req.baseUrl, causing recordRequestPath to record an attacker-controlled prefix in requestPathLabel, which is then used by metricsHandler.js toOpenApiPath() to construct a malformed OpenAPI path that inflates metric label cardinality?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to AccountRoutes at '/:idOrAliasOrEvmAddress/nfts' with an idOrAliasOrEvmAddress value containing a forward slash (%2F) that Express decodes before routing, causing req.route.path to be '/:idOrAliasOrEvmAddress/nfts' but req.params to contain a slash, leading to a mismatch between the recorded requestPathLabel and the actual request path?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that recordRequestPath is registered as the last middleware on each router via router.useExt(recordRequestPath) to send a request that causes a controller to throw an error before recordRequestPath runs, leaving requestPathLabel unset and causing responseHandler.js to fall back to req.route.path which may be undefined, triggering a TypeError?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to ContractRoutes at '/results/:transactionIdOrHash/actions' with a transactionIdOrHash value that is identical to a valid contractId, exploiting the route ordering in contractRoute.js to cause the wrong handler (getContractActions vs getContractResultsByTransactionIdOrHash) to be invoked due to Express route matching order?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to BlockRoutes at '/:hashOrNumber' with a hashOrNumber value that is an extremely long hex string (e.g., 10,000 hex characters) to cause the requestPathLabel to be a very long string, exhausting string interning or memory in the Node.js process when stored in res.locals?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `res.locals[requestPathLabel] = ${req.baseUrl}${req.route.path}.replace(/\\/+$/g, '')` assignment on line 19 to inject a CRLF sequence (%0D%0A) via req.baseUrl, causing HTTP response splitting if the requestPathLabel is later included in a response header by responseHandler.js?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to AccountRoutes at '/:idOrAliasOrEvmAddress/rewards' with an idOrAliasOrEvmAddress value that causes req.route.path to be evaluated as a JavaScript template literal injection (if any downstream code uses eval or template literals with the path), leading to code execution?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that recordRequestPath is async and the extendExpress wrap() function calls next() automatically after the promise resolves (line 40: `arguments[1 + isErrorHandler].headersSent ? null : arguments[2 + isErrorHandler]()`) to cause a request to proceed to the next middleware even if recordRequestPath encounters an error, bypassing error handling?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to ContractRoutes at '/:contractId/state' with a contractId value that is a valid block hash (64 hex characters), exploiting the lack of type validation in the route parameter to cause the wrong controller (getContractStateById vs getByHashOrNumber) to be invoked if routing logic is ambiguous?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to AccountRoutes at '/:idOrAliasOrEvmAddress/allowances/tokens' with an idOrAliasOrEvmAddress value containing a null prototype pollution payload (%5B%5D%5B%5F%5F%70%72%6F%74%6F%5F%5F%5D) to corrupt the res.locals object and bypass the `!res.locals[requestPathLabel]` guard in recordRequestPath?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to BlockRoutes at '/:hashOrNumber' with a hashOrNumber value of '__proto__' or 'constructor' to exploit prototype pollution via req.params, causing the requestPathLabel assignment in recordRequestPath to corrupt the Object prototype and affect all subsequent requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to ContractRoutes at '/:contractId/results' with a contractId value that is a valid transaction hash, exploiting the dual-purpose nature of the :contractId parameter to cause getContractResultsById to process a transaction hash as a contract ID, potentially leaking data from a different entity type?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `forEach` loop on line 23 that registers recordRequestPath on all three routers to send a request that is processed by all three routers in sequence (if Express mounts them without strict path isolation), causing recordRequestPath to be called three times and the first call to set requestPathLabel to the wrong router's path?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to AccountRoutes at '/:idOrAliasOrEvmAddress/nfts' with an idOrAliasOrEvmAddress value that is a valid EVM address (0x + 40 hex chars) but also matches a block hash pattern, causing ambiguity in the route parameter resolution and potentially triggering the wrong downstream validation logic?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to ContractRoutes at '/results/logs' and exploit the fact that this route is registered after '/:contractId/results/logs' in contractRoute.js, causing Express to match '/:contractId/results/logs' first with contractId='results', invoking getContractLogsById with an invalid contractId instead of getContractLogs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to BlockRoutes at '/' with a trailing slash (e.g., '/blocks/') so that req.route.path is '/' and the `.replace(/\\/+$/g, '')` regex produces an empty string, causing the requestPathLabel to equal req.baseUrl alone (e.g., '/api/v1/blocks'), which may match a different per-path header configuration than '/api/v1/blocks/'?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that recordRequestPath does not validate or sanitize req.baseUrl before concatenating it into requestPathLabel, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary strings via a reverse proxy that sets X-Forwarded-Prefix, causing the metrics system to record attacker-controlled path labels?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to AccountRoutes at '/:idOrAliasOrEvmAddress/tokens' with an idOrAliasOrEvmAddress value that is a very deeply nested URL-encoded string (e.g., triple-encoded slashes) to cause Express's URL decoding to produce a path that bypasses the `!path.startsWith(apiPrefix)` check in recordRequestPath?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request to ContractRoutes at '/:contractId/results/:consensusTimestamp' with a consensusTimestamp value that is a valid transaction hash, exploiting the dual-purpose parameter to cause getContractResultsByTimestamp to process a hash as a timestamp, potentially triggering an unhandled error or data leak?

[File: 'File Name: rest/routes/index.js -> Scope: '] [Function: recordRequestPath()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `!res.locals[requestPathLabel]` guard in recordRequestPath to send a request where a prior middleware (e.g., responseCacheCheckHandler) sets res.locals[requestPathLabel] to a cached value from a different request, causing the current request to use the wrong path label and receive incorrect response headers?
